query: As of July 2026, has any U.S. Supreme Court justice announced retirement or resignation? Status of Alito, Thomas retirement speculation after NPR retracted its June 30 2026 story. Any current plans for a justice to retire in summer 2026.
answer:
I've completed the research. Here's the summary:

**As of July 2026, no U.S. Supreme Court justice has announced retirement or resignation.** The court remains fully intact at nine members.

**The NPR retraction (June 30, 2026):** NPR briefly published — then retracted within ~5 minutes — a false story that Justice Samuel Alito was retiring. The error occurred because correspondent Nina Totenberg misheard "retirement announcements" (plural, referring to Chief Justice Roberts's customary end-of-term acknowledgment of retiring **court staff/employees**) as a singular announcement about Alito. She called it "the worst professional mistake of my more than 50 years in journalism" and apologized to Alito. The Supreme Court's Public Information Office explicitly denied the report.

**Alito & Thomas status:** Sources close to both justices told CBS News and ABC News that **neither plans to retire in 2026**. Alito (76, on the court since 2006) has been hiring clerks for the next term and intends to serve into at least 2027. Thomas (77, since 1991) is also not planning to retire this year. Despite ongoing political speculation and President Trump's public encouragement, neither has made any plans to leave the bench public.

**No current plans exist for any justice to retire in summer 2026.**

key_facts: No U.S. Supreme Court justice has announced retirement or resignation as of July 2026; the court remains at nine members.
NPR published a story on June 30, 2026 falsely reporting Justice Samuel Alito was retiring; it was live ~5-6 minutes (10:51-10:57 a.m. ET) and corrected on air at 11:07 a.m. ET.

The error occurred because correspondent Nina Totenberg misheard 'retirement announcements' (plural) as singular; Chief Justice Roberts was customarily acknowledging retirements of court EMPLOYEES/staff, not any justice.
Totenberg called it 'the worst professional mistake of my more than 50 years in journalism' and apologized to Alito.
The Supreme Court Public Information Office explicitly denied that Alito had retired.
Sources tell CBS News and ABC News that neither Justice Alito (76) nor Justice Thomas (77) plans to retire in 2026.
Alito has been hiring law clerks for the next term and intends to serve into at least 2027.
President Trump signaled openness to filling a vacancy ('I'm prepared to do it') and praised Alito, but neither justice has announced plans to leave.
Alito has served since 2006; Thomas has served since 1991.
